one_line_takeaway: Flipping just 48 Truck labels (0.5% of a 9,600-clip acoustic vehicle dataset) drives Truck->Car misclassification to 95.7% while leaving aggregate accuracy statistically unchanged, and the paper proves this stealth is a structural consequence of class imbalance (bounded by the target class's population fraction) rather than an artifact of this specific model or dataset.
key_finding: At p=0.5% (48 flipped Truck labels), the label-flip attack achieves ASR=95.7% (95% CI [88.4, 100], n=3 seeds) while test accuracy stays at 87.6%, identical to the clean baseline; at p=1.0% ASR=94.9% (accuracy 87.4%) and at p=2.0% ASR=95.7% (accuracy 86.4%) -- i.e., increasing poisoning rate beyond 0.5% does not meaningfully increase ASR, and aggregate accuracy never moves outside training-run noise. The analytic bound Delta_Acc_max <= beta is derived from the fact that a full collapse of a class contributes at most its own population fraction to aggregate accuracy loss; for Truck (beta ~3%), a 96% ASR reduction moves aggregate accuracy by only ~2.9%, below normal training-run variance. For the backdoor variant at p=0.5%, overall accuracy is 87.44%, Clean ASR = 94.87% and Triggered ASR = 94.87% (identical) -- the paper attributes this 'trigger-dominance collapse' to the fact that poisoning 0.5% of the full dataset consumes ~26% of the ~182 available Truck training samples, leaving too few clean Truck examples for the model to form any class representation independent of whether the patch is present.
core_contribution: The paper demonstrates targeted, near-undetectable training-data poisoning against a compact 2-D CNN acoustic vehicle classifier trained on the MELAUDIS urban-intersection dataset. It empirically establishes p*<=0.5% (48 flipped labels) as an effective poisoning rate for a Truck->Car label-flip attack, proves analytically that aggregate accuracy monitoring is structurally incapable of detecting attacks confined to a minority class (bounding the maximum detectable accuracy drop by the targeted class's population fraction beta), and identifies a new phenomenon it calls 'trigger-dominance collapse' -- where, for minority target classes, a spectrogram backdoor patch becomes functionally redundant because label-flipping alone already exhausts the class's discriminative signal, so triggered ASR equals clean (unpatched) ASR. The paper then proposes a trust-minimized ML pipeline defense using content-addressed hashing, Merkle-tree dataset commitment, and post-quantum (ML-DSA-65 / CRYSTALS-Dilithium3, NIST FIPS 204) signatures over each pipeline stage's output.
threat_model: Attacker has write access to any unsigned stage of the training pipeline (annotation CSVs, feature manifests, split files, or spectrogram-generation code) -- modeling a supply-chain adversary, a compromised annotation vendor, or a malicious insider. Attacker goal: induce the model to misclassify Truck as Car at inference time while keeping aggregate test accuracy statistically indistinguishable from a clean baseline, so the attack evades standard evaluation/monitoring.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) only a single compact CNN architecture was evaluated; ASR may differ for larger or more regularized models. (2) Truck has only ~182 training samples, so the high ASR at p=0.5% partly reflects the small absolute size of the target class rather than a universally low poisoning threshold. (3) The backdoor trigger tested is a fixed, visible 12x12 white-square patch; invisible or feature-space triggers may show different trigger-dominance dynamics. Additional grounding-note-level observations (not in the paper's own limitations section but evident from the text): the generalization claims to thermal/FLIR, RF spectrum sensing, radar, and LiDAR domains (Section VIII.A) are presented as reasoned extrapolation from the beta-bound argument, not as new experiments -- no data or results for those modalities are reported. The paper is a single-author preprint with a broken internal cross-reference ('Section ??' in Section VIII.A), and the defense architecture (Merkle commitment + ML-DSA-65 signing) is described and partially implemented (src/melaudis/merkle.py, with a demonstrated abort message on tampering) but no end-to-end performance or security evaluation of the defense itself (e.g., signing/verification overhead, key management, adoption cost) is presented.

datasets_used: MELAUDIS (Mel Spectrogram dataset for Urban Acoustic Vehicle Identification and Surveillance, Melbourne intersections; ~9,600 single-vehicle clips, 6 classes: Car 84.4%, Tram 6.3%, Truck 2.7%, Bus 2.7%, Motorcycle 2.6%, Bicycle 2.3%)
quantitative_results: Label-flip p=0.5% (48 records): accuracy 87.6%, ASR 95.7% (95% CI 88.4-100%, n=3 seeds)
Label-flip p=1.0% (96 records): accuracy 87.4%, ASR 94.9% (95% CI 83.8-100%)
Label-flip p=2.0% (192 records): accuracy 86.4%, ASR 95.7% (95% CI 92.0-99.4%)
Backdoor attack p=0.5%: overall accuracy 87.44%, Clean ASR 94.87%, Triggered ASR 94.87% (equal -> trigger-dominance collapse)
Analytic bound: Delta_Acc_max <= beta (targeted-class training fraction); for Truck beta~3%, a 96% within-class ASR yields only ~2.9% aggregate accuracy movement
At p=0.5%, poisoning consumes ~26% of Truck's ~182 training samples
baselines_compared: Clean (unpoisoned) trained CNN baseline (87.6% test accuracy, ~0% Truck->Car ASR)

No prior poisoning-attack baseline from other papers is re-run; comparison is internal across the three poisoning rates (0.5%, 1%, 2%) and between label-flip-only vs. label-flip+backdoor-patch variants
